event.title: thirty years, for military and strategic reasons, Ladakh was pardy opened

data.event: After being closed to outsiders for thirty years, for military and strategic reasons, Ladakh was pardy opened in 1974, and immediately became an important destina­ tion for the serious student of Buddhism and Tibetology.

event.title: 1974, and immediately became

data.event: After being closed to outsiders for thirty years, for military and strategic reasons, Ladakh was pardy opened in 1974, and immediately became an important destina­ tion for the serious student of Buddhism and Tibetology.

event.title: the nine years since it was opened

data.event: In spite of its many features of interest, no comprehensive account of Ladakh has been published in English in the nine years since it was opened, and authentic and up-to-date information is hard to come by.

event.title: studied up till now

data.event: In it, I have tried to place on record some of the important facts about a part of the world which has been litde studied up till now, and which has often been regarded as no more than an undifferentiated appendage of old Tibet.

event.title: lived for two years

data.event: I lived for two years in Leh, and during that time formed a deep affection for the land and its people.

event.title: acquired in the line of business a wide knowledge of the country as it is today

data.event: My husband, Sayeed Rizvi, was Development Commissioner, or head of the district administra­ tion; as well as sharing my affection/or Ladakh, he acquired in the line of business a wide knowledge of the country as it is today, a good deal of which rubbed off on me.

event.title: 1976-8) Ladakh was a single district, in area India’s

data.event: In our day (1976-8) Ladakh was a single district, in area India’s largest, in population one of its smallest.

event.title: June 1979 it was split

data.event: In June 1979 it was split in two, the town of Kargil becoming the headquarters of a district of the same name.

event.title: took place; even now

data.event: Most of this book was written before the bifurcation took place; even now it is hard for me to think of Ladakh as more than a single district.

event.title: centuries bound

data.event: centuries bound the often sharply dif­ ferentiated regions of the two districts together.

event.title: lived for two years

data.event: Acknowledgements M y first thanks must be to the people of Ladakh, among whom I lived for two years, and who treated me with unfailing kindness and generosity.

event.title: the duties usually expected of the bahu, during long winter

data.event: My parents-in-law, Mr and Mrs S.Q. Rizvi, not only absolved me of the duties usually expected of the bahu, during long winter sojourns with them, but also came to baby-sit and take over my domestic responsibilities, when I left Mussoorie for my return trip to Ladakh.

event.title: n the twelve years

data.event: Preface and Acknowledgements to the Second Edition I n the twelve years since this book was published there have been many changes in Ladakh.

event.title: attended a seminar on ‘Tourism and the Environment’ in Leh in September 1995

data.event: This was commented on by a very senior official of the Government of India who attended a seminar on ‘Tourism and the Environment’ in Leh in September 1995.

event.title: speeded up to an unprecedented degree during the following decade

data.event: The political process speeded up to an unprecedented degree during the following decade, coming to an unfortunate

event.title: inaugurated in September 1995

data.event: head with the agitation of 1989 in Leh, and eventually finding a kind of resolution with the establishment of the Leh Autonomous Hill Development Council, inaugurated in September 1995.

event.title: So many changes in such a short time

data.event: So many changes in such a short time: it all cried out to be documented; and so did the virtual explosion in Ladakh studies that has taken place since the late 1970s.

event.title: was closed to the Western world for 25 years

data.event: Ladakh was closed to the Western world for 25 years after Independence; it is one of the last regions where Tibetan Buddhism is practised in its original setting; some even of its major valleys have only in the last one or two decades been incorporated into the modern world by the construc­ tion of roads fit for wheeled traffic.

event.title: is practised in its original setting; some even of its major valleys have only in the last one or two decades

data.event: Ladakh was closed to the Western world for 25 years after Independence; it is one of the last regions where Tibetan Buddhism is practised in its original setting; some even of its major valleys have only in the last one or two decades been incorporated into the modern world by the construc­ tion of roads fit for wheeled traffic.

event.title: was thrown open to outside visitors in 1974

data.event: As a result, since it was thrown open to outside visitors in 1974 it has become a magnet for scholars in many disciplines.

event.title: meets in conference every two years

data.event: Today there is a flourishing International Association of Ladakh Studies, which meets in conference every two years, and there is a move to set up an Institute of Ladakh studies in Leh, to ensure Ladakhi participation in the study of their own country, and access to the fruits of this growing corpus of research.

event.title: a monumental work of co-operative scholarship, with whose long-awaited appearance late in 1994

data.event: I’m sure no one will take it amiss if I make special mention here of only one book, a monumental work of co-operative scholarship, with whose long-awaited appearance late in 1994 Ladakh studies may be said to have come of age: Himalayan Buddhist Villages, edited by John Crook and Henry Osmaston, a report on a multi­ disciplinary study undertaken in Zanskar just before it was opened up to motor traffic.

event.title: is given a musical welcome by lamas from the roof 54 The town gate of Leh, 1904

data.event: Illustrations Black and white A masterpiece of road design and engineering, the Hangru Loops — known popularly as the Jalebi Bends — carry the Kargil—Leh road down from Lamayaru to Khaltse 9 On the lip of a gorge — the only vestige of the torrent that sculpted it being a trickle of water in the pebbly stream-bed — perches the ancient fort of Heniskot 27 A snow-leopard in magnificent pelage looks inquiringly at researchers who have just fastened a radio-collar around its neck 44 In a sketch by a Ladakhi artist early this century, a prince arriving at the gate of a gompa is given a musical welcome by lamas from the roof 54 The town gate of Leh, 1904.

data.event: A trader from Hoshiarpur, whose family traded between Yarkand and the Punjab from the beginning of the century, stands at the door of his shop in Leh’s main bazaar.

event.title: n the twenty-odd years

data.event: Introduction: Where Many Roads Meet I n the twenty-odd years since Ladakh was opened up to out­ siders, the ‘aura of mystery and inaccessibility’ that I referred to at the start of my introduction to the earlier edition of this book has worn a little thin.

event.title: nine centuries expanded

data.event: The borders of the independent kingdom that Ladakh was for some nine centuries expanded and shrank according to the relative power of its rulers and those of neighbouring countries.

event.title: included all the areas that are part of the region today

data.event: But for most of that time they included all the areas that are part of the region today which, at the level of the ruling family and the aristocracy, were bound together by a series of marriage al­ liances.

event.title: antedates the Potala by some thirty years

data.event: The Palace at Leh antedates the Potala by some thirty years and is on a much more modest scale; but the two buildings are clearly in the same tradition as are the great monasteries of both countries.

event.title: common use in Ladakh today

data.event: Water-mills for grinding barley, seemingly identical to those in common use in Ladakh today are described by George Bogle, the first Briton to make an extended stay in Tibet, as early as 1774-5; the amazingly sophisticated system of irrigation by means of channels contouring along the mountain slopes from streams often kilometres away is also current over the whole region, where precipitation is minimal and people must depend for water on the melting of the snow on the heights.

event.title: shifted east­ wards, and by the thirteenth century AD

data.event: But the centre of religious authority eventually shifted east­ wards, and by the thirteenth century AD the Ladakh religious establishment, along with those of the other countries on Tibet’s borders, had been relegated to a position of subordination tc rhe great monasteries of central Tibet, which thus became the focusej of a religio-cultural empire extending from Mongolia to Bhutan, and from Ladakh to the Koko-Nor.

event.title: the closing years

data.event: 4 LADAKH written to describe the monastic festivals anywhere in Ladakh in the closing years of the twentieth century.

event.title: started in 1959

data.event: The impression is confirmed by the autobiographies of former Tibetan monks which have been published in western languages since the Tibetan, diaspora started in 1959, and which to anyone familiar with the Ladakh monastic scene are Rill of familiar echoes.

event.title: years of discretion, retire

data.event: Peissel is also one of the few travellers to have described the custom known as khang-bu, or ‘little house’, in which the elders of a family, as soon as the eldest son has reached years of discretion, retire gracefully from participation in affairs, and taking only enough of the property for their own sustenance, yield the head­ ship of the family to him.

event.title: existed over the whole Tibetan cultural area till 1959

data.event: Recent anthropological and sociological research indicates that a complex pattern involving varying forms of both these customs existed over the whole Tibetan cultural area till 1959, and perhaps still survives in remote parts of Nepal.

event.title: remained current in the villages of Buddhist Ladakh until at least the early 1940s

data.event: Both remained current in the villages of Buddhist Ladakh until at least the early 1940s, when polyandry and inheritance by primogeni­ ture, which is part of the same system of family organization, were made illegal by the then Government of Jammu and Kashmir.

event.title: developed over the centuries

data.event: Situated at the centre of a network of important routes, Leh developed over the centuries into a major entrepot.

event.title: reflected even now

data.event: This gave the capital something of a cosmopolitan air, reflected even now in the mixed racial composition of its population; while the speech of the town, a derivative of Tibetan as everywhere in Ladakh, is enriched by an admixture of foreign words, mainly Persian and Urdu.

event.title: adds a unique excitement to summer

data.event: It has been kept alive in the Leh area by the settlement of Shia Muslims, originally from Baltistan, at Chushot, a village 15 kilometres up the Indus; but it generates wild enthusiasm among Muslims and Buddhists alike, and adds a unique excitement to summer in Leh.

event.title: the Dogra conquest of 1834

data.event: On the political level, the Dogra conquest of 1834—42 brought Ladakh within the orbit of India; and though till 1959 the lamas con­ tinued to look to Lhasa for religious inspiration, the new genera­ tions of secular Ladakhis started going to Srinagar and the universities of British India for higher education.

event.title: brought Ladakh within the orbit of India; and though till 1959

data.event: On the political level, the Dogra conquest of 1834—42 brought Ladakh within the orbit of India; and though till 1959 the lamas con­ tinued to look to Lhasa for religious inspiration, the new genera­ tions of secular Ladakhis started going to Srinagar and the universities of British India for higher education.

event.title: became an integral part of the Indian polity in 1947

data.event: Thus the modern world, first brought in a small way by the devoted work of Moravian missionaries, made its more permanent mark via India; Ladakh became an integral part of the Indian polity in 1947 with the accession to India of the princely state of Jammu and Kashmir of which it formed a part.

event.title: the 1960s, when the road link with Kashmir was built

data.event: After that, but more especially since the 1960s, when the road link with Kashmir was built, it has been involved in the nation-wide programme of development and mod­ ernization which has been the hallmark of independent India’s economic effort.

event.title: forced its way in with the Young- husband Expedition in 1904

data.event: Old Tibet turned in on itself, refusing access to all outsiders except those traders and pilgrims whose journeys were sanctioned by ancient and uninterrupted usage, until the modern world forced its way in with the Young- husband Expedition in 1904.

event.title: written, and up to about 1987

data.event: When this book was first written, and up to about 1987, this effectively meant taking what used to be called the ‘Treaty Road’ from Srinagar, via Kargil and over the three passes: Zoji-la, Namika-la and Fatu-la.

event.title: That year however the old traders’

data.event: That year however the old traders’ route from Manali to Leh was made fit for motor traffic, and thrown open to tourists; and after another three years the growth of militancy in Kashmir deprived the route from Srinagar of its attraction for all but the most intrepid, not to say foolhardy, of visitors.

event.title: thrown open to tourists; and after another three years

data.event: That year however the old traders’ route from Manali to Leh was made fit for motor traffic, and thrown open to tourists; and after another three years the growth of militancy in Kashmir deprived the route from Srinagar of its attraction for all but the most intrepid, not to say foolhardy, of visitors.

event.title: strenuous activity for at least another couple o f days

data.event: (Air travellers are advised absolute rest for 36 hours after their arrival, and should avoid strenuous activity for at least another couple o f days to allow for acclimatization.) The flight itself, however, over the Great Himalaya and the Zan­ skar range, is spectacular.

event.title: crosses four passes, three of them over 4800 metres, is open for no more than three-and-a-half months

data.event: The route from Manali, which crosses four passes, three of them over 4800 metres, is open for no more than three-and-a-half months, from the end of June to mid-October.

event.title: now enters

data.event: Although Lahul, the district which the road now enters, is not actually part of trans-Himalaya, since the Great Himalaya remains to be crossed, it lies in the rain-shadow of the Pir Panjal, and the view ahead could well be a trans-Himalayan landscape.

event.title: days of the traffic in grain and wool between Kulu and the high-altitude pastures of Ladakh and Tibet, the Lahul people profited

data.event: In the days of the traffic in grain and wool between Kulu and the high-altitude pastures of Ladakh and Tibet, the Lahul people profited from their situation mid-way between the two end-points of the trade by acting as carriers.

event.title: the discovery about a century ago

data.event: Since the discovery about a century ago that conditions in the district are ideal for growing potatoes, the farmers no longer confine their operations to subsistence agriculture.

event.title: ’s southern flank are the summer

data.event: APPROACHES 13 Great Himalaya’s southern flank are the summer destination of the transhumant Gaddi shepherds, part of whose trek to and from their traditional grazing runs may take them along the motor road.

event.title: Now the valley narrows

data.event: Now the valley narrows, and the road remains high on the constricting walls of its gorge, passing the two Buddhist monasteries of Tayul and Gemur.

event.title: the 1950s an important meeting-place for traders from Zanskar, Kulu and Rupshu, as well as Lahul itself, where salt and wool from the high-altitude lands ahead were bartered

data.event: On again, still following the course of the Bhaga, to the bridge at Patseo, till the 1950s an important meeting-place for traders from Zanskar, Kulu and Rupshu, as well as Lahul itself, where salt and wool from the high-altitude lands ahead were bartered for food- grains, tea and other essential commodities brought up from Kulu and Zanskar.

event.title: known as ‘cashmere’, the raw material of the Kashmir shawl industry and now

data.event: The Chang-pa goats are the source of pashm, better known as ‘cashmere’, the raw material of the Kashmir shawl industry and now in demand world-wide as one of the softest, warmest and most delicate woollen fibres.

event.title: Now it hairpins up

data.event: Now it hairpins up the stony mountainside to reach the watershed of the Great Himalaya, which it crosses by the famous Bara-lacha-la (4892 metres), a pass possibly unique in having a tri-junction at its summit, with a trail taking off towards the south-east, down the Chandra valley and eventually over into Spiti.

event.title: the start of the route over the Chang-la to the Pang-gong Lake and on, either to western Tibet, or up the Shayok gorge on the winter

data.event: This valley is the start of the route over the Chang-la to the Pang-gong Lake and on, either to western Tibet, or up the Shayok gorge on the winter caravan route to central Asia.

event.title: Now the valley begins

data.event: Now the valley begins to broaden out, and right in the centre rises a huge rocky eminence, crowned by the impressive pile of Stakna Gompa.

event.title: now passes

data.event: The road now passes through villages, whose fields almost merge into each other: Ranbirpura, carved out of the desert during the reign of Maharaja Ranbir Singh towards the end of the nineteenth century; and

event.title: defies belief that in November 1948

data.event: It almost defies belief that in November 1948, during the first Indo-Pakistan war, a column of tanks made its way through a near-blizzard, up this punishing ascent, to attack and dislodge the Pakistani forces who held the pass.

event.title: summer route, whose alignment is still visible in places, zig-zagged up

data.event: The summer route, whose alignment is still visible in places, zig-zagged up parallel to and a litde below today’s motor road; in winter the crossing was made by scrambling over the masses of snow that filled the ravine.

event.title: Today, when travellers are totally dependent on the wheel, the snow that covers

data.event: Today, when travellers are totally dependent on the wheel, the snow that covers the Zoji-la to depths of up to ten metres in winter forms an insuperable barrier, closing the pass for at least six months of the year.

event.title: winter, whenever the snow was not actually falling

data.event: But well within living memory, mail-runners and porters from Dras carrying back-loads of trade goods, were cross­ ing more or less regularly throughout the winter, whenever the snow was not actually falling.

event.title: winter’s

data.event: All along the road at this point are poles marked off in metres from the ground, to measure the depth of the winter’s snow.

event.title: find a way in through the gap of the Zoji-la, with the result that the ground is deep in snow for several months

data.event: The Great Himalaya forms a barrier to the entry of moisture-laden clouds to the rest of Ladakh, but here they find a way in through the gap of the Zoji-la, with the result that the ground is deep in snow for several months in winter.

event.title: winter than at Dras, the climate allows

data.event: At about 2750 metres, and with a considerably shorter winter than at Dras, the climate allows of a lush growth of fruit trees and crops, including wheat, wherever there is water.

event.title: — or alternatively the rich golds and reds of their autumn

data.event: Areas within reach of irrigation being a small part of the whole are thus intensively cultivated, two harvests a year being taken; and the brightness of the green fields and trees — or alternatively the rich golds and reds of their autumn colours — looks almost unreal, as if super­ imposed on the immediately adjoining bare brown hillsides.

event.title: climbs a huge mound of alluvial deposit to the Khurbathang plateau, which was till a few years ago

data.event: Across the Suru river from Kargil, the road climbs a huge mound of alluvial deposit to the Khurbathang plateau, which was till a few years ago naked of all but the low creeping vegetation seen on most of the seemingly barren mountainsides of Ladakh.

event.title: year 1600, that a Balti army lay

data.event: It must have been here, around the year 1600, that a Balti army lay in wait for the king of Ladakh, Jamyang Namgyal, after perhaps tricking that inept ruler into crossing the Fatu-la in winter; and having outmanoeuvred and defeated him proceeded to overrun central Ladakh; and it was here that forty years later his son Sengge Namgyal, Ladakh’s most famous king, met a force sent from Kashmir by command of the Great Moghul himself, the Emperor Shah Jahan.

event.title: forty years later his son Sengge Namgyal, Ladakh’s

data.event: It must have been here, around the year 1600, that a Balti army lay in wait for the king of Ladakh, Jamyang Namgyal, after perhaps tricking that inept ruler into crossing the Fatu-la in winter; and having outmanoeuvred and defeated him proceeded to overrun central Ladakh; and it was here that forty years later his son Sengge Namgyal, Ladakh’s most famous king, met a force sent from Kashmir by command of the Great Moghul himself, the Emperor Shah Jahan.

event.title: laid down when a far greater volume of water than now

data.event: They are alluvial or lacustrine deposits, laid down when a far greater volume of water than now filled this narrow valley; their tortured forms are the result of millennia of wind erosion.

event.title: now on the left, as the road loops and bends

data.event: APPROACHES 23 — on the other side, not of the narrow Wanla river, but of the Indus itself — appearing now on the right hand, now on the left, as the road loops and bends its way down.

event.title: Now the traveller reaches

data.event: Now the traveller reaches the village of Khaltse, and is entering the heartland of Ladakh; the outward signs of Buddhism are everywhere.

event.title: held out for three years

data.event: In the 1680s, the fort held out for three years when besieged by the combined Tibetan-Mongol army that invaded Ladakh on the orders of the fifth Dalai Lama.

event.title: now turns

data.event: The road now turns away from the river for the last time — passing, rather unexpectedly, a modern Sikh temple dedicated to Guru Nanak — and crosses one more barren plateau.

event.title: carried a far greater volume of water than they do now

data.event: The Indus meanders in a number of channels through the wide bed it cut for itself in the alluvium brought down by its tributary torrents, at a period when both mainstream and tributaries carried a far greater volume of water than they do now.

event.title: were formed over the next 45 million years

data.event: The ranges were formed over the next 45 million years by the buckling and folding of the landmasses being squeezed together; as the Indian plate ground its way under the Asian one, the land all along the line of impact was lifted thousands of metres into the air.

event.title: o f years

data.event: At the same time, over the millions o f years since the uplift o f the ranges, the opposite process of erosion has also been at work, so that their original structure has been modified by the displacement and deposit o f material some o f which has in its turn solidified again into mountains.

event.title: see today

data.event: Water, and the movement of glaciers, are the later forces which have sculpted the landscape into the shape we see today.

event.title: climbed in 1953

data.event: Nun-Kun was first climbed in 1953 by a French group which approached from Kishtwar; later expeditions have approached the massif from the Suru valley.

event.title: joins the Indus at Marol — in Baitistan, which is now

data.event: The Suru, after receiving the combined waters of the Dras and Shingo rivers a short distance north of Kargil, joins the Indus at Marol — in Baitistan, which is now on the Pakistan side of the Line of Control — at an altitude of little more than 2700 metres; but its headwaters rise high in the mountains of the Zanskar range and the Great Himalaya, where they emerge from the glaciers east of Nun-Kun; they come together to form the mainstream on the plain of Rangdum at about 3800 metres.

event.title: Today’s travellers, having no such compelling reasons, seem

data.event: Today’s travellers, having no such compelling reasons, seem reluctant to linger there; even those bound for Zanskar usually push through without stopping.

event.title: winter’s

data.event: The winter’s heavy snowfall on the northern flank of the Great Himalaya being converted by the sun of summer into water abundant enough to irrigate not only extensive fields, but also plantations of willow and poplar which make it positively lush — by Ladakhi standards at least.

event.title: summer into water abundant enough to irrigate not only extensive fields, but also plantations of willow and poplar which make

data.event: The winter’s heavy snowfall on the northern flank of the Great Himalaya being converted by the sun of summer into water abundant enough to irrigate not only extensive fields, but also plantations of willow and poplar which make it positively lush — by Ladakhi standards at least.

event.title: will ripen during the brief summer

data.event: There is no certainly that crops will ripen during the brief summer; and the ground remains under snow to a depth of two metres or more during the four or five months of winter.

event.title: remains under snow to a depth of two metres or more during the four or five months

data.event: There is no certainly that crops will ripen during the brief summer; and the ground remains under snow to a depth of two metres or more during the four or five months of winter.

event.title: now, although it has been on the main ‘highway’

data.event: Even now, although it has been on the main ‘highway’ into Zanskar since 1980, the people’s lifestyle seems to have changed only marginally.

event.title: remained at least till the 1980s

data.event: In the villages, where the population are exclusively Buddhist, life and religious practice remained at least till the 1980s much as they always had; and the same is true of the minuscule setdement of Sunni Muslims in Padum.

event.title: Today, essential commodities and consumer goods which were formerly transported

data.event: Today, essential commodities and consumer goods which were formerly transported laboriously over the passes by horse caravan, can be brought in easily by truck; the Government has opened a chain of ration shops here as elsewhere in Ladakh.

event.title: suffers heavy winter

data.event: Like the Suru valley, Zanskar suffers heavy winter snowfall; the Pensi-la opens only in June, and is blocked again by mid-October, and even in the central plain the growing season lasts only from the end of April to the second half of September.

event.title: opens only in June

data.event: Like the Suru valley, Zanskar suffers heavy winter snowfall; the Pensi-la opens only in June, and is blocked again by mid-October, and even in the central plain the growing season lasts only from the end of April to the second half of September.

event.title: lasts only from the end of April

data.event: Like the Suru valley, Zanskar suffers heavy winter snowfall; the Pensi-la opens only in June, and is blocked again by mid-October, and even in the central plain the growing season lasts only from the end of April to the second half of September.

event.title: winter, when the passes are blocked

data.event: In winter, when the passes are blocked by snow, the frozen waters of the Zanskar river,

event.title: ’s only link with the outside world, although today

data.event: PROFILE OF A LANDSCAPE 31 locally known as the Chadur, form Zanskar’s only link with the outside world, although today, in the mid-1990s, there are per­ sistent rumours that the Zanskaris’ long-standing demand for an all-weather road along the Chadur is likely to be met by the end of the century.

event.title: centuries (as no doubt earlier too) when the glaciers tributary

data.event: There have been occasions in the last couple of centuries (as no doubt earlier too) when the glaciers tributary to the uppermost Shayok have ad­ vanced so as to block completely the trough through which the river rampages on its course.

event.title: occurred in 1929

data.event: The last of these cataclysms occurred in 1929, since when the glaciers appear to be in retreat.

event.title: centuries, such were the compulsions of com­ merce, that a major trade route was established

data.event: Yet, over the centuries, such were the compulsions of com­ merce, that a major trade route was established between Leh and the oasis towns of Sinkiang— mainly Yarkand and Khotan.

event.title: took no less than thirty days

data.event: But whichever route the caravans took, the journey involved the crossing of a minimum of four passes and took no less than thirty days.

event.title: winter snowfall on the mountains which, stored

data.event: The main source of water is the winter snowfall on the mountains which, stored for months as snow and ice and glaciers, runs off in summer as meltwater streams which are tapped to irrigate the fields.

event.title: months as snow and ice and glaciers, runs off

data.event: The main source of water is the winter snowfall on the mountains which, stored for months as snow and ice and glaciers, runs off in summer as meltwater streams which are tapped to irrigate the fields.

event.title: summer as meltwater streams which are tapped

data.event: The main source of water is the winter snowfall on the mountains which, stored for months as snow and ice and glaciers, runs off in summer as meltwater streams which are tapped to irrigate the fields.

event.title: see today

data.event: The glaciers we see today, however impressive, are but vestigial remains of those which hollowed out — for instance — the Nubra and Zanskar valleys, filling them with ice up to 500 metres thick.

event.title: recent years —

data.event: The current tendency of the glaciers all over the Himalaya and the Karakoram seems to be to retreat; how this may be linked with the increasing rainfall of recent years — whether, for example, they are both symptoms of global warming — and whether the one could conceivably cancel the other out, are questions that only extended studies over decades will be able to answer.

event.title: questions that only extended studies over decades

event.title: see today

data.event: The salt lakes on the plateaux to the south-east appear to be the vestiges of a far more extensive system of fresh­ water lakes; and great alluvial and lacustrine deposits elsewhere — most dramatically below Lamayuru — prove the existence in past ages of quantities of water out of all proportion to the small streams we see today.

event.title: now perhaps a small stream finds

data.event: Huge gorges through the mountains can only have been cut by terrific torrents rushing down them over untold ages; now perhaps a small stream finds its way down the centre of the chasm — a stream whose insignificance is mocked by the grandeur of the water-wrought formations at whose foot it trickles.

event.title: now come

data.event: Any substantial increase in the amount of cultivable land can now come about only by the intervention of those develop­ ment agencies which have at their disposal huge quantities of money and technical expertise.

event.title: were implemented during the late 1980s

data.event: Two such projects, indeed, were implemented during the late 1980s and early 90s, one each close to Leh and Kargil; as a result, the amount of cultivable land in the two districts has gone up by almost one-third.

event.title: are relieved by the green of the growing crops, or in autumn

data.event: Along every watercourse, natural or man-made, clings a strip of natural or cultivated vegetation; and wherever water is relatively abundant, the ubiquitous greys and browns of a parched landscape are relieved by the green of the growing crops, or in autumn by the gold of harvest and the crimson leaves of the apricot trees.

event.title: ’s great rivers, should be a desert, dependent for its water on the storage of the winter

data.event: It seems to be an irony of nature that this land, through which flows in its upper reaches one of the world’s great rivers, should be a desert, dependent for its water on the storage of the winter’s snow in the glaciers and its release by the sun of summer.

event.title: winter, even when the shade temperature remains

data.event: Similarly, in the warmest weeks of summer, nights are cool; conversely in winter, even when the shade temperature remains well below freezing, the sun still gives real warmth.

data.event: Late in May, for instance, when the crops are being sown around Leh, they are already half-grown at Khaltse; a month-and-a-half later, there will be golden fields down the Indus, while at Leh the barley is still green, and the harvest two months distant.

event.title: contemplates the merchant travellers of past centuries

data.event: Less obvious on the ground is the geographically coherent pattern of parallels, which is so clear from the map; it is therefore with wonder and respect that one contemplates the merchant travellers of past centuries who, without the resources of modern surveying techniques and transport, worked out the hard way the best possible routes across this unpromising terrain.

event.title: appears before the leaves on the apricot trees at the beginning of spring

data.event: PROFILE OF A LANDSCAPE 41 flowers of tall tamarisk bushes; clumps of dwarf iris, their form no less perfect than that of their full-grown cousins in more temperate climes; the virginal white blossom that appears before the leaves on the apricot trees at the beginning of spring: all this fragile loveliness acquires a heart-breaking poignancy from its setting of bare earth and rock and the savage grandeur of the ridges.

data.event: Perhaps the greatest surprise among the bird life is the brown-headed gull, which is seen along the Indus in summer, and which breeds along the margins of the Star-tsapuk Tso, Rupshu’s fresh-water lake.

event.title: 1976, when two pairs were observed

data.event: Twenty years after the first sightings, the species seems to be holding its own.

event.title: winter home of the pairs found

data.event: The black­ necked crane is a high-altitude bird, with a short migration range; but nobody has yet discovered the winter home of the pairs found in Ladakh in summer.

event.title: see the winter

data.event: Birds that see the winter out, at least at the altitude of Leh, are the sparrow, the crow, the magpie, the grey tit, and the rather rare ibisbill.

event.title: recorded by expeditions to Ladakh in the 1980s

data.event: The total number of bird species recorded by expeditions to Ladakh in the 1980s and early 90s adds up to an astonishing 225; they include, besides those already mentioned, sandpipers, stilts, stints, eagles, owls, bee-eaters, larks, mynas, warblers, thrushes, tits, and no fewer than fourteen different finches.

event.title: seemed reasonably plentiful in September 1995

data.event: 46 LADAKH on the endangered list include, besides the black-necked crane, the bar-headed goose, the golden eagle, the Tibetan sandgrouse, the Tibetan partridge, the Tibetan snow-cock, and the chukor — although happily this last seemed reasonably plentiful in September 1995, being spotted in numerous small flocks along the Leh-Khaltse highway.

event.title: licences a year

data.event: Under the Maharaja’s rule forty licences a year were issued; but the nature of the terrain made it impossible to enforce any restriction, so it may be assumed that a lot of poaching went on.

event.title: is reported that as late as 1958

data.event: Even so, it is reported that as late as 1958 there were sizeable flocks of shapu (the Ladakh urial) and bharal, the so-called blue sheep, and even a few goa, or Tibetan gazelle.

event.title: 1970 the shapu and bharal were clearly much reduced

data.event: By 1970 the shapu and bharal were clearly much reduced, though still present in a few areas, while it was not possible to spot the goa at all.

event.title: now reduced

data.event: The bharal is found along almost the whole of the Great Himalaya, on both sides of the crest-line, from Ladakh to Sikkim; although now reduced, it is still the most numerous of the ungulates, and is not immediately endangered either in Ladakh or in its range as a whole.

event.title: consider the ibex a sacred animal, they have had a tradition of hunting it; in the past

data.event: Although the Brokpa of western Ladakh consider the ibex a sacred animal, they have had a tradition of hunting it; in the past however this would not have been on a large enough scale to make a serious dent in its numbers.

event.title: now found

data.event: It is one of the rarest of all the mountain sheep, and is now found only in small isolated populations; its numbers in Ladakh are estimated at about one thousand.

event.title: the past been mercilessly trapped and hunted

data.event: Those animals which have it in sufficient quantities to make it worth the trouble and risk of collecting, have in the past been mercilessly trapped and hunted, and Cunningham tells us that ibex in great numbers used to be ‘snared at night and shot in the grey dawn of the morning, when they venture down to the streams to drink’.

event.title: prized for centuries

data.event: But it is the Tibetan antelope or chiru, whose local name is stos, which pro­ duces the wool prized for centuries above all others for shawls, known as toosh, or even shahtoosh — royal toosh.

event.title: today’s conservation awareness makes

data.event: The fineness and warmth of this delicate fibre are remarkable; however, today’s conservation awareness makes it evident that to drape oneself in a shahtoosh shawl is no less immoral than to wear a sealskin coat or a karakul cap.

event.title: the times of the Mughals, but it is only in this century

data.event: The weavers of Srinagar have been making shahtoosh shawls since at least the times of the Mughals, but it is only in this century that the number of chiru has been drastically reduced.

event.title: 1900 tell

data.event: Explorers in western Tibet around 1900 tell of herds numbering many thousands on the broad grasslands there; more surprisingly, tra­ veller after traveller on the Leh-Yarkand trade route record their presence in considerable numbers on both sides of the Karakoram Pass, a region where there is an almost total absence of vegetation.

event.title: made the journey in 1926

data.event: Theodore and Kermit Roosevelt, who made the journey in 1926 and one of whose tasks was the collection of specimens for the Chicago Natural History Museum, were quite unable to figure out how they could possibly subsist.

event.title: killed were as fat as butter-balls.’1 Today

data.event: CREATURES OF THE WILD 49 thrive on this meagre diet, for those we killed were as fat as butter-balls.’1 Today, the strategically sensitive area up to the Karakoram Pass is controlled by the army, who probably think they have better things to do than to concern themselves with the status of the chiru, although a proposal for a sanctuary has been made.

event.title: the past this made

data.event: The chiru is famous for following the same migration routes year after year (in the past this made it easy for the local Chang-pa to trap and kill them), and there is believed to be a small breeding population which wanders in from western Tibet to the Aksai Chin in summer.

data.event: Even more threatened is the Tibetan gazelle or goa, a small, slender and graceful animal, of which there used to be sparse populations in Rupshu, Hanle and Changchenmo, but which now seems to be headed for local extinction.

data.event: Although its beautiful pelt, whose pattern of white, grey and black blends almost to invisibility into the moun­ tain landscape of rock and snow, commands a high price in the illegal fur market, it is too elusive to be the target of systematic poaching; those individuals that fall to the poacher’s gun are probably the victims of opportunistic killing, at least in Ladakh.

data.event: It is believed that snow-leopards exist in all the high mountain areas of Ladakh, though the density varies according to the availability of prey species; today there may still be some two hundred individuals in the region.

event.title: many years spent

data.event: Ladakh Past 4 The Old Kingdom T he history of Ladakh was first researched and reconstruc­ ted by the German scholars Emil and Hermann von Schlagintweit, Karl Marx and A.H. Francke. Marx and Francke were members of the Moravian mission, and combined an academic approach to Ladakh’s past with a knowledge of the country and its traditions that could only have been gained as the result of many years spent living in close and constant touch with its people.

event.title: was published with an English translation by Francke in 1926

data.event: For the most part they used rock inscriptions and the records available to them in Ladakh or originating there, the main body of which, known collectively as the Ladakh Chronicle, was published with an English translation by Francke in 1926, in Volume II of his Antiquities of Indian Tibet.

event.title: had already published in 1907

data.event: He had already published in 1907 his History of Western Tibet in which, however, the results of his later research are missing.

data.event: In 1977 Professor Luciano Petech, of the Istituto Italiano per il Medio ed Estremo Oriente, published his book, The Kingdom of Ladakh c.

event.title: now know

data.event: They may at an early period have colonized the whole of the country we now know as Ladakh.

event.title: exist in Ladakh today

data.event: The pockets of Dard population that exist in Ladakh today seem to consist of the descendants of later immigrants from the Dard homeland.1 The earliest Tibetans to migrate into Ladakh may have come as nomadic herdspeople, after the fashion of those who still roam the bare upland plateaux of Ladakh, as well as over the border in Tibet; but wholesale immigration may have waited for the establishment of, first the loose suzerainty of Tibet over the countries contiguous to it on the west — what we now know as Ladakh, and probably Baitistan too — in the eighth and ninth centuries; and more positively for the establishment of a Tibetan dynasty in Ladakh in the middle of the tenth century.

event.title: the closing of the passes in winter

data.event: Given the slowness of travel through the mountains and the long delays imposed by the closing of the passes in winter, some must have stayed on, and others left half-Ladakhi children behind them.

event.title: entered the western parts of what is now

data.event: It is certain that Buddhism first entered the western parts of what is now Ladakh, not from Tibet, but from Kashmir, perhaps as early as the first or second century AD, when there are indications that parts of the country may have been incorporated in the Kushan empire.

event.title: centuries AD

data.event: This movement would have been only one aspect of the close cultural and religious links between Ladakh and north-west India particularly Kashmir which may have been maintained for close on a thousand years, from the Kushan era, of the first and second centuries AD, to the twelfth century— well into the period of rule by an originally Tibetan dynasty.

event.title: 100 years after the assassination, that Lang-dar-ma’s

data.event: It was during this prolonged period of turmoil, and probably be­ tween 75 and 100 years after the assassination, that Lang-dar-ma’s great-grandson Nyima-Gon, accompanied by representatives of some of the haute noblesse of central Tibet, migrated westwards and established his rule, probably in the provinces immediately to the east of present-day Ladakh.

event.title: think of as Ladakh today

data.event: 58 LADAKH centre well to the east of present-day Ladakh; and though he allotted to his eldest son, Pal-gyi-Gon, the area from the Zoji-la roughly to the present eastern border approximately — the area we think of as Ladakh today, minus the province of Zanskar — this seems to have implied what Petech calls a theoretical right of sovereignty, the actual conquest being left to Pal-gyi-Gon.

event.title: had taken place as a result of the earlier political relationship was now

data.event: Whatever Tibetanization had taken place as a result of the earlier political relationship was now to be reinforced and confirmed by an immigrant ruling class, eventually making of Ladakh a country inhabited by a mixed population, the predominant racial strain of which was Tibetan, speaking a form of Tibetan, and, while politically independent, subordinate to Tibet in religion and religious culture.

event.title: had surely suffered in the political turmoil of the centuries

data.event: In the first two centuries or so after the conquest, however, the kings of Nyima-Gon’s dynasty, intent on establishing Buddhism in their new dominions, looked not to Tibet, where previously established Buddhist traditions had surely suffered in the political turmoil of the centuries since the assassination of Lang-dar-ma, but to north-west India, particularly to Kashmir.

event.title: po’s time or soon after (the eleventh and twelfth centuries

data.event: Tradition con­ nects his name with a number of monasteries in Kinnaur and Spiti, the most impressive of which is at the village of Tabo; in Ladakh there are the remains of foundations dating from Rin-chen-zang- po’s time or soon after (the eleventh and twelfth centuries) at Chigtan in Kargil district, at Lamayuru, and at Nyarma, Basgo and Alchi in the Indus valley.

event.title: now a collection of ruins, are definitely attributed

data.event: Of all these, only Tabo and Nyarma, which is not far from Tikse and is now a collection of ruins, are definitely attributed to Rin-chen; but it is reasonable to assume that others of the same period came into existence, if not with his direct participation, then at least under his inspiration.

event.title: spent two years

data.event: He spent two years at Tholding, then the capital of Guge, where, on his arrival, the only monk who did not rise to his feet in respectful greeting was the aged Rin-chen-zang-po, who must have felt his nose badly out of joint.

data.event: In these two events we have, perhaps 150-200 years after Yesh-es-od, the first indications that Ladakhi Buddhists were looking to central Tibet rather than to India for inspiration.

event.title: ascended the throne about 1616

data.event: Sengge Namgyal probably ascended the throne about 1616 as a minor, his mother acting as regent.

event.title: years of his reign, his authority faced

data.event: In the early years of his reign, his authority faced a challenge from his younger brother Norbu Namgyal, who even seems to have succeeded in wresting the throne from him for a year or two before his own premature death.

event.title: established a mission in 1626

data.event: His conquest of Guge, recorded in the Chronicle and in the biography of Stag-tsang-ras-pa (which is a more reliable source for the events of this reign than the Chronicle) is confirmed by the evidence of the Jesuits who had, improbably enough, established a mission in 1626 at Tsaparang, the capital of Guge.

event.title: three years the head of the mission, described

data.event: Antonio de Andrade the founder and for two or three years the head of the mission, described in detail the background and actual events of the Ladak­ hi conquest, which took place in 1630; indeed it was this which, depriving the Jesuits of the support extended by their patron, the king of Guge, brought their activities in Tsaparang to an end.4 The Guge king was carried off into honourable exile in Ladakh; Guge was later handed over to a younger son of Sengge Namgyal.

event.title: Bernier’s visit in 1663

data.event: According to the French traveller Francois Bernier, Sengge, in his pique at this defeat, closed the trade route from Kashmir, and forbade any person to enter his dominion from that direction, the embargo lasting up to the time of Bernier’s visit in 1663.

event.title: his death in 1642

data.event: Indeed, the decline in Ladakh’s fortunes that set in after his death in 1642 may reasonably be attributed to the ‘economic suicide’ (in Professor Petech’s phrase) committed by Sengge Namgyal.

event.title: few years religious missions to Tibet carried

data.event: As well as the monasteries, Sengge’s reign saw religious life in Ladakh enriched by a number of mani walls — which may have been introduced by Stag-tsang-ras-pa — and statues, including the gigantic Buddha-image in the temple at Shey; while every few years religious missions to Tibet carried rich offerings in the form of gold, silver, turquoises and precious stuffs.

event.title: years when perhaps conditions were more unsettled than the Chronicle and other sources actually indicate

data.event: In earlier years when perhaps conditions were more unsettled than the Chronicle and other sources actually indicate, Shey, which has a magnificent strategic position, would have been the obvious choice for the capital, and the same might hold good for the period of the Muslim invasions.

event.title: happened only three years

data.event: In spite of the memory of the Balti invasion only a very short time before Sengge’s birth, it may be that his vigorous personality, and his achievements at home and abroad before the disaster at Bodh Kharbu, which happened only three years before his death, imparted a sense of security and self-confidence to the Ladakh polity, one result of which was the development of mercantile Leh at the expense of embattled Shey.

event.title: died at Hanle in 1642

data.event: Sengge Namgyal died at Hanle in 1642, on his way back from an expedition against the Mongol occupiers ofTsang, the Tibetan province adjoining Guge to the north-east, who were making threatening moves against Ladakh.

event.title: 1647 a partition was effected

data.event: In 1647 a partition was effected in which Ladakh proper went to the eldest son, Deldan Namgyal; Guge to the middle one, a lama known only by his religious name, Indrabodhi Namgyal; and Spiti and Zanskar to the youngest, Demchog Namgyal.

event.title: lasted till 1694

data.event: The custom of the Ladakhi and other Tibetan dynasties was that the heir-apparent, on reaching years of discretion, should be as­ sociated with the work of government, so that in many cases two reigns seem to overlap.

event.title: was lifted in 1993

data.event: Zanskar, with its scanty population and its secluded position, was supplied with salt by the Chang-pa of Rupshu, whose twice- yearly trips almost as far as Padum continued till well into the 1980s.

event.title: went every three years

data.event: The Lo-pchak Mission from Leh to Lhasa went every three years, carrying gold, saffron and textiles as offerings to the Dalai Lama, as well as gifts for other important lamas.

event.title: was taken on by the Government of Jammu and Kash­ mir, it was for many years

data.event: Interest­ ingly, there was no stipulation that these should be Buddhists, and after the loss of Ladakh’s independence, when the responsibility of sending it was taken on by the Government of Jammu and Kash­ mir, it was for many years conducted by one particular family of Muslim merchants named Radhu.

event.title: held for many years

data.event: It may be safely assumed that the fortunes of this family were substantially augmented by the near-monopoly they held for many years over the Lo-pchak, but they were already prosperous and successful traders when they took it up.

event.title: the beginning of this century their business empire extended

data.event: By the beginning of this century their business empire extended not only between Leh and Lhasa, but to Shigatse and Gartok in Tibet, Yarkand and Kashgar in Sinkiang, and Srinagar in Kashmir.

event.title: was said in 1906

data.event: Branches in all these cities were run by members of thef extended family, whose trade was said in 1906 to bring in a net annual profit of Rs 25,000 — a very pretty sum at that time.

event.title: this century there was only one other family in Leh doing business on anything like the same scale, Buddhists whose capital was derived

data.event: During this century there was only one other family in Leh doing business on anything like the same scale, Buddhists whose capital was derived from their extensive properties, including thousands of head of livestock kept for them by Chang-pa in western Tibet.

data.event: The leader of the Cha-pa mission, whose title was actually Zhung-stong, meaning official trader, was given a ceremonial welcome by the representative of the former royal family, as part of the Losar (New Year) ceremonies held in the Leh Palace every year up to the 1940s.

event.title: held in the Leh Palace every year up to the 1940s

event.title: Travel time between the two centres was two-and-a-half months

data.event: Travel time between the two centres was two-and-a-half months, at least for the slow-moving caravans, and much of the way was through uninhabited country.

event.title: this century, the establishment of Kalimpong as the principal centre of trade between India and Tibet, together with the development of modern systems of communication in India, had rendered

data.event: By the second quarter of this century, the establishment of Kalimpong as the principal centre of trade between India and Tibet, together with the development of modern systems of communication in India, had rendered the direct route between Leh and Lhasa practically irrelevant.

event.title: remained brisk till the 1950s

data.event: Trade with western Tibet in salt, foodgrains and pashm remained brisk till the 1950s, tailing off after the Chinese occupation of Tibet and finally coming to an end about I960.

event.title: the early 1930s, and the last Lo-pchak set off

data.event: But there were no private caravans to Lhasa after the early 1930s, and the last Lo-pchak set off from Leh in 1942.

event.title: was established in this century

data.event: Up to the middle of the eighteenth century India and Tibet maintained a busy com­ mercial relationship via the Kathmandu valley; and after a lull of nearly a century-and-a-half a similar trade was established in this century, via Kalimpong and Sikkim.

event.title: took the caravans a full month

data.event: What made Ladakh’s situation unique was its mid-way position between trading centres so distant from each other that it took the caravans a full month from either end to reach Leh; and also the fact that the traffic along this most punishing of routes appears to have carried on for centuries without a break, the only interruption in the three hundred years of its recorded history apparendy being the twenty-five years or so following Sengge Namgyal’s closure of the border with Kashmir.

data.event: As the trans-Karakoram trade dwindled in the 1930s and 40s, some of them opened retail shops in Leh, several of which are still extant.

event.title: two months travel

data.event: Yarkand and Khotan, its termini in Sinkiang, were important staging posts on the southern branch of the fabled ‘silk route’ between China and the Mediter­ ranean, and appalling though the hardships of the Ladakh-Kara- koram route between the Punjab and Yarkand might be — a journey involving a minimum of two months travel time — there was in fact no feasible long-term alternative.

event.title: centuries the caravans congregated

data.event: Thus over the centuries the caravans congregated at Leh to prepare for the crossing of the Karakoram, or to recover from it; and gradually there, and at the other staging posts along the different lines of march, a network of serais and other facilities was established, which further rein­ forced the advantages of the route.

event.title: other radical changes of this century

data.event: HAZARDOUS TRAILS 105 ebbed and flowed, sensitive like all long-distance trades to politi­ cal developments, but strong enough to survive all vicissitudes until the revolutions and other radical changes of this century destroyed its very base.

event.title: toiled over the Karakoram to Yarkand and Kashgar in 1870

data.event: The two diplomatic missions that toiled over the Karakoram to Yarkand and Kashgar in 1870 and 1873, with the aim of concluding a commercial treaty with Yakub Beg, the de facto ruler; the high hopes with which they set out, and their total lack of success, constitute one of the more bizarre and entertaining episodes in the history of the British imperial adventure.1 Even if successful, the effort would have been in vain, for by the end of the 1870s the Chinese were back, this time for good.

event.title: survived only a few years

event.title: fourteen years he was actively engaged

data.event: For fourteen years he was actively engaged in the trans-Karakoram trade, himself regularly struggling across the fearsome passes with his caravans.

event.title: 1888, that he met

data.event: It was on one of these expeditions, in 1888, that he met his death, not from cold or exposure, but (an unnecessary additional hazard, it might seem, on this most punishing of moun­ tain routes) from the bullet and knife of an assassin.

event.title: centuries when silk was actually passing

data.event: Although the Leh—Yarkand route was not (contrary to popular misconception) the Silk Route, it was pre­ sumably one of the Silk Route feeders in the early centuries when silk was actually passing through Yarkand and the other oasis towns of south Sinkiang on its way from China to the Mediterranean.

event.title: the 125 years of the Karakoram route’s

data.event: And during the 125 years of the Karakoram route’s recorded history, silk was in fact an important item of commerce, going from India to central Asia in the form of Banaras brocades and other woven fabrics, and from central Asia to India mainly in the form of yarn for the weaving towns of the Punjab.

event.title: about a hundred years, the Punjabi merchants made

data.event: For about a hundred years, the Punjabi merchants made enormous profits from the import of charas, this was the only commodity that the Government took an active interest in, monitoring its movement from the moment it entered Ladakh till it was dis­ tributed to the retailers from special warehouses in Hoshiarpur and Rawalpindi, and taking their cut via duties.

event.title: now that Nubra is open to outside visitors —

data.event: A few enterprising traders in central Ladakh and Nubra acquired camels for local use; they failed to thrive in the Leh area, but conditions in Nubra seem to suit them and a small population survives there, proving their worth as pack- animals and — now that Nubra is open to outside visitors — being exploited as a tourist attraction.

event.title: summer route were more or less heavily glaciated: the Khardung-la just behind Leh, the Saser-la, which took

data.event: Three of the passes on the summer route were more or less heavily glaciated: the Khardung-la just behind Leh, the Saser-la, which took the route over the Saser Spur from the Nubra to the upper Shayok valley, and the Sanju-la over a ridge of the Kun-lun in Sinkiang.

event.title: open a trail over the passes at the beginning of the season

data.event: Yak were also used to push through the snow and open a trail over the passes at the beginning of the season.

event.title: take today

data.event: The direct route up from the Punjab was the one that so many visitors take today, via Mandi, the Kulu valley, the Rohtang Pass, Lahul, the Bara- lacha, Lunga-lacha and Tag-lang Passes, to Upshi on the Indus, and thence down the river to Leh.

data.event: This was Moorcroft and Trebeck’s route in 1820; it carried a busy traffic until the early years of this century, and was in occasional use by Punjabi traders right up till the 1950s.

data.event: In the tract between Dras and Kargil, one of Ladakh’s poorest regions, the only thing that lifted most families above the level of bare subsistence was the carrying trade between Srinagar and Leh, of which, in the first half of this century, these people had a virtual monopoly.

data.event: Today the trails are deserted, except where they coincide with the arteries of modern traffic; otherwise, almost the only movement along them is of trekkers and climbers pitting their strength against the grandeur of the mountains and serviced by the successors of the traders of old.

event.title: 70 years, which contrasts

data.event: 1 1 6 LADAKH As in every other region of India, Ladakh’s population has grown during the present century, rising from 63,519 by the census of 1901, to 105,291 in 1971, a moderate increase of 66 per cent in 70 years, which contrasts with the alarming 25 per cent increase between 1971 and 1981.

event.title: 1901, it means

data.event: Assuming that the amount of cultivable land today is roughly the same as that available in 1901, it means that at that date each square kilometre had to support about 300 persons; while by 1941 with the population registering almost exacdy 76,000, the pressure on the fields had gone up to nearly 350 per square kilometre.

event.title: the first half of this century seems

data.event: Research into the complex pattern of the inter-regional subsistence trades in the first half of this century seems to show that, while there was no large-scale organized import of foodgrains, those peasant traders whose circuit included Kashmir and Kishtwar, regions south of the Great Himalaya, did regularly bring back not only rice, but also maize and a selection of coarse grains for everyday subsistence, like buckwheat.

event.title: estimate in 1847

data.event: But there was in addition a high rate of infant and child mortality, showing that in the past the harsh­ ness of the environment acted as a check on the population*, so that it at no time outstripped the resources necessary to provide every individual with the means of survival.

event.title: benefited from its position on the main caravan route to Yarkand, many of its fields in the old days

data.event: It too benefited from its position on the main caravan route to Yarkand, many of its fields in the old days being given over to the cultivation of lucerne (known in Ladakhi as ol), which the villagers sold at a consid­ erable profit to the passing caravans as fodder for the horses.

event.title: is removed at the end of winter

data.event: Ladakhis use dry closets built a few feet above the ground, in which the refuse, falling through a hole, is collected in a lower chamber, from which it is removed at the end of winter and taken out to the fields.

event.title: the grip of winter

data.event: After the harvest, the fields are ploughed once before the earth hardens so much in the grip of winter as to become unworkable.

event.title: Weeding starts about three weeks

data.event: Weeding starts about three weeks after the seed has been sown; as well as direcdy benefiting the growing grain, it supplements the supply of fodder available for the livestock.

event.title: carried over, perhaps, from the days

data.event: The work of weeding and watering is done mosdy by women, who apart from ploughing can and do undertake every kind of activity connected with agriculture — a custom carried over, perhaps, from the days when a considerable number of the able-bodied men left their homes in summer to go trading, or employed as porters and pony-drivers with the caravans.

event.title: the early 1980s, before the arrival of the motor road had made

data.event: Studies in Zanskar in the early 1980s, before the arrival of the motor road had made any appreciable difference to traditional practice, established that ‘grain yields in Zangskar are much better than most, and nearly as high as those obtained from intensive high input farming in Europe’; in some villages this was achieved without recourse to rotation or fallowing.5 In the villages, as well as in Chang-thang where the nomads lead a pastoral life, almost every family keeps some livestock.

event.title: remain, but enough is now

data.event: Many questions remain, but enough is now known to establish the fact that their system of migration and utilization of the pastures is a highly sophisticated one which takes full account of natural processes and is in accord with the scientific principles of grasslands ecology.

event.title: takes place before the winter

data.event: Meat is rather a by-product of the necessary process of culling the flocks, which takes place before the winter so as to avoid wasting scarce fodder on animals which have outlived their usefulness.

event.title: show unusual strength of the heart and lungs, which few outsiders, even after years

data.event: In particular, to cope with the rarefied air they have to breathe, they show unusual strength of the heart and lungs, which few outsiders, even after years of acclimatization, will be able to match.

event.title: will feel intolerably oppressed by the summer

data.event: The nomadic herds­ man from Chang-thang, used to an altitude of 4500 metres, will feel intolerably oppressed by the summer heat in Leh, and the thickness of the atmosphere, and is said to think of Srinagar much as eighteenth-century Europeans thought of Calcutta — as some­ where terminally hot and unhealthy, from where they will be lucky to return alive.

event.title: 55 years of age was over 10, while the figure for other districts in Jammu and Kashmir varied

data.event: One result of this is that at the other end of the scale, longevity has been in the past greater in Ladakh than in other places with a kinder environment, the census figures (1961) showing that the percentage of the population over 55 years of age was over 10, while the figure for other districts in Jammu and Kashmir varied from 5.5 to 8 per cent.

event.title: much time as possible out of doors, even in winter

data.event: Men and women alike spend as much time as possible out of doors, even in winter, gratefully savouring the sun’s warmth.

event.title: the 1970s and early 80s, when the tourist influx led

data.event: Of recent years, this system has been regaining the popularity it is said to have lost in the 1970s and early 80s, when the tourist influx led to a craze for all things Western, at the expense of traditional values and methods.

event.title: worn smooth by years

data.event: As for the manufacture of homespun cloth, the spindle is often no more than a sturdy twig, about 20 centimetres long and the thickness of a child’s finger, worn smooth by years of use, and dangling free, or with its spin supported in a tiny saucer.

event.title: now, and the thread of the weft is passed

data.event: Outside the Government-sponsored weaving centres, the flying shutde is unknown even now, and the thread of the weft is passed laboriously from hand to hand — a procedure which necessarily limits the cloth to narrow widths.

event.title: left for years

data.event: Moorcroft also describes a system whereby walls are constructed along the mountainsides towards their base, and left for years to trap the alluvium brought down by the melting snows, thus laying the foundation for future cultivation.

event.title: summer; but in the villages they stay

data.event: Of course animals have to be looked after in winter as in summer; but in the villages they stay close to their quarters, which are usually on the ground floor of the dwelling-houses, so tending them is primarily a matter of giving them their water and fodder, no more.

event.title: The main work of this season

data.event: The main work of this season is weaving, which is done on local looms often set up on the sunny rooftops.

event.title: come largely to a halt in winter

data.event: Apart from these, even the new kinds of economic activity brought by the processes of ‘development’ and ‘modernization’ come largely to a halt in winter.

event.title: the work available during the six months

data.event: But increased prosperity, largely derived from the work available during the six months’ construction season on various projects of the public works programme, in Leh as well as Kargil, or on the continuous process of road maintenance, has nearly put an end to this.

event.title: winter is punctuated and given

data.event: In the Buddhist areas, winter is punctuated and given colour by the annual festivals of the various gompas, most of which take place at intervals during the cold season.

event.title: is dictated by the cycle of the seasons

data.event: In Nubra, Zanskar and the Suru valley, as in the Sham Ilaqa and Chang- thang, the rhythm of life is dictated by the cycle of the seasons; ploughing, seed-time and harvest; the rearing of animals, birth and death among the flocks, and their wanderings in search of pasture.

event.title: operates between man, his physical environment, and the rhythm of the seasons

data.event: Traditionally, it has been a life lived close to nature, modified by only the simplest of technological devices, where an extraordinarily delicate balance operates between man, his physical environment, and the rhythm of the seasons.

data.event: The one spoken at Dras now has a strong admixture of Kashmiri words and idioms, while the speech of the Da-Hanu people has been influenced by their Ladakhi neighbours and contains many Tibetan-derived expressions.

event.title: were made illegal by the Government of Jammu and Kashmir in the early 1940s

data.event: Both poly­ andry and inheritance by primogeniture were made illegal by the Government of Jammu and Kashmir in the early 1940s.

event.title: had reached years

data.event: When the eldest son had reached years of discretion, and had married and produced an heir, the parents retired gracefully to a small house adjoining the main property, taking one or two animals with them, and retaining only as much land as they needed to support themselves.

event.title: devolved on the eldest son, who was now

data.event: All the responsibilities of managing the property devolved on the eldest son, who was now recognized as the legal owner; the parents had no further claim on it.

data.event: An interesting and perhaps unique feature of village life in Ladakh is the pha-spun, an institution that even now gives solidarity

event.title: days before the interventions of Government provided

data.event: In the days before the interventions of Government provided, as they undoubtedly have, some minimal security for the less well-off, the rich were not above exploiting the poor.

event.title: hold good today

data.event: Many of Drew’s observations hold good today; the differences point to a distinct increase in prosperity since he wrote.

event.title: Today, there may be a very few households in the poorer regions where tea, liberally laced

data.event: Today, there may be a very few households in the poorer regions where tea, liberally laced with butter, is not a daily drink; but generally speaking tea is no longer considered a luxury, but a necessity of life.

event.title: winter most Ladakhis (with the exception of the Da-Hanu Dards, and possibly of the Chang-thang nomads) make

data.event: But even in winter most Ladakhis (with the exception of the Da-Hanu Dards, and possibly of the Chang-thang nomads) make an effort to keep themselves accept­ ably clean; while in summer bathing is commonplace and every stream is the scene of vigorous laundry work, particularly during the early weeks with a winter’s grime to be washed out of heavy woollen clothes.

event.title: discard in summer

data.event: Nowadays tht goncha— which men in cosmopolitan Leh often discard in summer in favour of Western-style dress — usually conceals, not only a full set of warm underclothing, but shirt, trousers and sweater as well; the women’s goncha too is worn over a complete salwar-kameez suit.

event.title: will use a variety of material for their gonchas, even, lightweight synthetics for summer

data.event: The well-to-do will use a variety of material for their gonchas, even, lightweight synthetics for summer wear.

event.title: carried her fortune on her head; over the years

data.event: The Ladakhi woman actually carried her fortune on her head; over the years, as family resources permitted the purchase of more stones, it became an heirloom, passed from mother to daughter.

event.title: figured Chinese silk: a reminder of the caravan trade through which, in the old days

data.event: Her best goncha is often in heavy figured Chinese silk: a reminder of the caravan trade through which, in the old days, such luxury goods were imported from great distances.

event.title: was ‘in’ this season

data.event: Fashions change, even in Ladakh, and what was ‘in’ this season may be quite out of style the next.

event.title: is hardly seen today

data.event: The chuba, the more stream­ lined and elegant Tibetan gown, which was frequently chosen for semi-formal wear by the ladies of Leh’s ^lite at the time the earlier edition of this book was written, is hardly seen today; and both the perak and the hat — without which no respectable woman would have stepped out of her house in the 1970s — are now confined to stricdy formal occasions.

event.title: now confined

event.title: was not observed at all in the late 1970s

data.event: This was not observed at all in the late 1970s; but fifteen years later, it has made a comeback — not indeed for normal wear, but for the troupes of dancers who perform at archery festivals and other social oc­ casions.

event.title: summer they discard

data.event: In summer they discard it in favour of an outift rather like the fashionable ‘Pathan’ garb: a cotton knee-length shirt over baggy trousers cut like a salwar, and topped by a loose waistcoat.

event.title: winter snowfall, the pattern of flat-roofed houses —

data.event: It is a little surprising to note that in Dras, the Suru valley and Zanskar, all of them subject to heavy winter snowfall, the pattern of flat-roofed houses — which originated, presumably, in the low-precipitation valleys of central Ladakh and

event.title: appears to be a satisfactory enough solution in winter

data.event: This appears to be a satisfactory enough solution in winter; but these roofs are far from rain-proof, and with the evident possibility of a change of climate involving increased precipitation, it may become necessary for future builders in Ladakh to rethink their roofing methods.

event.title: the main departure from tradition in the houses of even the well-to-do today

data.event: For the most part, however, apart from the introduction of electricity, the main departure from tradition in the houses of even the well-to-do today is the extensive use of glass.

event.title: Now, in the towns and those villages which are easily accessible by road, not only do many of the houses have their windows glazed

data.event: Now, in the towns and those villages which are easily accessible by road, not only do many of the houses have their windows glazed in the usual way, but every reasonably prosperous home has a ‘glass room’: a room one or two of whose outside walls, facing south or west, are glass from top to bottom.

event.title: is screened off, even in deepest winter

data.event: The sun is so strong that if the cold air is screened off, even in deepest winter it will make a glass room warm enough so that no other form of heating is needed during the day.

event.title: the introduction of glass, and even now

data.event: Before the introduction of glass, and even now in the villages, the centre of the Ladakhi home was the one room which in the nature of things would always be warm — the kitchen.

event.title: now in the villages, the kitchen served

data.event: In the old days, and even now in the villages, the kitchen served as sleeping quarters for the whole family in the cold months of winter.

event.title: the efficient use of that now

data.event: For many years now, the wood- or dung-burning stove has been supplemented in the houses of the well-to-do by the primus- type stove burning kerosene, which makes possible the efficient use of that now indispensable appliance, the pressure-cooker — a boon for high-altitude cooking.

event.title: evenings, the glass-room is warmed

data.event: On winter evenings, the glass-room is warmed by the bokhari, the cylindrical free-standing stove used in Kashmir, where it burns wood; here it is adapted to give a fiercer heat by burning hard coke or kerosene oil.

event.title: evenings, there are story-telling sessions, when the villagers gather

data.event: In most villages, during the long winter evenings, there are story-telling sessions, when the villagers gather to listen to stories drawn from their rich folklore, told by that one among them whose gifts have made him the acknowledged tale-teller of their group.

event.title: seem to be little more than excuses for a summer

data.event: The same cannot be said of archery; and as far as the Leh area is concerned, the so-called archery festivals seem to be little more than excuses for a summer party.

event.title: decked out in its Sunday

data.event: Boldly patterned shamianas shelter a crowd decked out in its Sunday best, the men sober yet stylish in their smartest gonchas, the women colourful with their brocade mantles and heavy strings of jewellery.

event.title: the archers practice for the competition throughout the winter

data.event: The archery season in Kargil is in March, the slack season before the spring ploughing, and the archers practice for the competition throughout the winter.

data.event: It seems to have been the early Muslim rulers who first introduced it into India, and it was popular at Akbar’s court; but it died out, except among the ruling families of some Rajput states, and was unknown to the British during the early days of their rule.

data.event: The sportsmen of the Raj first played polo in Calcutta about 1860, having discovered it in Manipur.

data.event: In 1935, Peter Fleming saw in Hunza a game practically identical with what is today known in Ladakh as Balti-style polo.
