event.title: saw at Mulbekh in 1848

data.event: A traveller lucky enough to see polo played in one of the villages of the interior may still see something like the game Cunningham saw at Mulbekh in 1848, when twenty players on each side pursued the ball up and down a field 360 metres long and 75 broad.

event.title: records the laying-out of a polo ground in Leh in 1885

data.event: Although the Ladakh Chronicle records the laying-out of a polo ground in Leh in 1885, travellers’ accounts show that the game was being played up and down the main bazaar until well into the twentieth century.

event.title: played in 1906

data.event: Here is an account of a game played in 1906: A game of polo is composed of all the sportsmen who like to join.

event.title: remains current in Ladakhi polo today

data.event: (Fraser, The M arches o f Hindustan, pp. 120—1) The practice of starting and resuming play by tossing the ball and hitting it in a single action from mid-field while galloping full tilt towards the opposing goal remains current in Ladakhi polo today.

event.title: laid out the first royal polo ground, in the Murtse garden below Leh (now

data.event: Some authorities say that it was brought to Ladakh by Sengge Namgyal whose mother was a Balti princess, early in the seventeenth century, and that it was Sengge who laid out the first royal polo ground, in the Murtse garden below Leh (now the site of the Defence Ministry’s Field Research Laboratory).

event.title: 21 March polo is played

data.event: Chushot is the only village in central Ladakh with its own polo ground, where every year on 21 March polo is played to celebrate Nauroz, the Persian (and Shia) New Year.

event.title: does not mean that today

data.event: This does not mean that today there is any lack of enthusiasm for the game among the Leh Buddhists — quite the contrary; interest is universal, and there are a fair number of Buddhists among the front-rank players.

event.title: today played

data.event: Apart from the immediate neighbourhood of Leh, polo is today played only in those parts of the district that have a Muslim population, such as the villages of Turtuk and Bogdang in Nubra, down the Shayok river to the north-west.

event.title: the game in the immediate post-Independence years

data.event: In Kargil district, the austere rule of the Muslim clerics to some extent put a damper on the game in the immediate post-Independence years; but it survived particularly in Dras, where it is said that the various villages can put as many as 24 teams in the field, and of recent years it has even made a comeback in Kargil and Chigtan.

event.title: decades of the century, polo revived

data.event: Languishing in and around Leh in the middle decades of the century, polo revived from the mid-1970s, thanks to positive steps taken by the district administration to encourage it.

event.title: today, it is a slightly modified form of the original game, but still it makes

data.event: As played today, it is a slightly modified form of the original game, but still it makes few concessions to international polo.

event.title: an army pursuit; but whereas in the late 1970s

data.event: The presence of the Army gives a sharpened interest to the game, for polo in post-Independence India has been almost exclusively an army pursuit; but whereas in the late 1970s the army used to field teams made up of players of the international game — who no doubt were sporting enough to adopt the local style of play — today it is observed that they prefer to confront like with like, so that the army teams are almost exclusively drawn from the local unit, the Ladakh Scouts.

event.title: lasted till the 1950s

data.event: The early-established system of novices being sent to monasteries in central Tibet to get their higher qualifications lasted till the 1950s, and was surely the most effec­ tive of the various channels through which Tibet’s religious culture was transmitted to Ladakh.

event.title: the present time as it is of past centuries

data.event: With the exception of whatever paint­ ings and images remain in the earliest monasteries — those of Rin-chen-zang-po’s time, of which Alchi is the prime example — all the works of art in the gompas of Ladakh reflect the Tibetan style, and this is as true of the work of the present time as it is of past centuries.

event.title: is carried on even today

data.event: Religious painting is a living tradition, in the sense that it is carried on even today; but it is carried on strictly within the bounds laid down by an iconography that has ceased to develop.

event.title: traded between Yarkand and the Punjab from the beginning of the century

data.event: A trader from Hoshiarpur, whose family traded between Yarkand and the Punjab from the beginning of the century, stands at the door of his shop in Leh’s main bazaar.

event.title: GENIUS 157 trace down the centuries

data.event: THE NATIVE GENIUS 157 trace down the centuries have either been abandoned, like the presumed Kashmiri style so amazingly exemplified at Alchi, or absorbed into a single style, from which no departure is permis­ sible, and of which the largest component is the Tibetan tradition.

event.title: appear now

data.event: When one thinks of some of the detail among the earlier wall- paintings — particularly the little scenes of secular life that appear now and then, especially at Alchi, also the court scene in which Tashi Namgyal’s portrait appears in the Gon-Kbang at Namgyal Tsemo — then the present state of reli- gious art, which means in effect its state for the past two or three hundred years, seems by comparison to be lacking in richness, and hidebound by tradi­ tion.

event.title: Four months’ journey notwithstanding, there was a constant exchange of per­ sonnel between the monasteries of Tibet and Ladakh, Tibetan monks

data.event: Four months’ journey notwithstanding, there was a constant exchange of per­ sonnel between the monasteries of Tibet and Ladakh, Tibetan monks bringing the dogmas and traditions received at the centre of this religious empire, and Ladakhi ones travelling to the centre to absorb them.

event.title: meant that, in the old days

data.event: Ladakh’s posi­ tion at the centre of an important network of trade routes meant that, in the old days, whatever people’s requirements beyond the basic necessities of life might be, they could be met by imports; thus there was little call for the development of indigenous in­ dustry.

event.title: continues even today

data.event: 158 LADAKH cloth, and in the villages this continues even today.

event.title: summer’s

data.event: In the winter sun, or the summer’s shade, the weaver sets up his simple loom and for a fee weaves the thread spun by the women of all the village households into the pattu which is universally the poor people’s clothing.

event.title: ’s most famous product, the warm soft winter

data.event: Ladakh’s most famous product, the warm soft winter under-coat of the pashmina goat, what used to be known as ‘shawl-wool’, was never in the past entrusted to the local people for any kind of processing beyond the most basic preliminary cleaning, which involved merely picking out the ad­ mixture of coarse hairs belonging to the goat’s shaggy outer coat.

event.title: the past entrusted

event.title: now, when not all the pashm leaves

data.event: Even now, when not all the pashm leaves Ladakh raw, without value added, the best ‘Ladakhi pashmina’ shawls can hardly compare with genuine ‘cashmere’.

event.title: Today there are a few private co-operatives of local women, some of them trained

data.event: Today there are a few private co-operatives of local women, some of them trained in Kashmir, whose product is a whole lot finer than what was available even in the 1980s.

event.title: was introduced about a hundred years ago

data.event: It was introduced about a hundred years ago by the Moravian missionaries, and Ladakhi women knit European-style with the wool wound round the fingers of the left hand, not the right as in Britain and elsewhere in India, and among the Tibetans.

event.title: today it is the first stage on the all-weather road planned

data.event: Formerly this village could be reached — except during deep winter when the solidly frozen waters of the river form a pathway — only by a strenuous march over a mountain pass from Alchi; today it is the first stage on the all-weather road planned as a link between central Ladakh and Zanskar.

event.title: this day, the popula­

data.event: To this day, the popula­ tion of the village consists of four extended families, whose lha or tutelary deity is the Nepali (Hindu) god Mahadeva; all the male members practise the ancestral craft.

event.title: now, in the accelerating tempo of the modern world, it remains

data.event: The beauty of the Ladakhi dance is that it is a living tradition, and even now, in the accelerating tempo of the modern world, it remains an important part of every Ladakhi Buddhist’s training in the social graces.

event.title: days, when the representatives of the Namgyal dynasty were kings in more than name, and held

data.event: In the old days, when the representatives of the Namgyal dynasty were kings in more than name, and held their royal state at Leh and Shey, indeed right up to the time when the old order finally passed away a mere half-century ago, the nobility of Ladakh would gather on the spacious roof of Leh Palace on special oc­ casions, to celebrate with song, music and dance the glories of the dynasty and of Ladakh’s history.

event.title: today is still much the same as that which the Namgyal kings and their courtiers saw

data.event: The ketuk-chenmo, the spacious roof of the palace, is still there; and though the twentieth century impinges at many points, the prospect before the visitor today is still much the same as that which the Namgyal kings and their courtiers saw, and celebrated in song.

event.title: includes a body of unwritten poetry whose richness is only now

data.event: The cycle of Ketuk-chenmo songs is also a pointer to the strength of an oral tradition which includes a body of unwritten poetry whose richness is only now getting the scholarly attention it deserves.

event.title: years of the twentieth century, the Moravian missionaries Karl Marx and A.H. Francke laid

data.event: In the early years of the twentieth century, the Moravian missionaries Karl Marx and A.H. Francke laid the foundation of Ladakh studies, and Francke published versions of the Kesar Saga, the national epic, as well as collections of songs and proverbs.

event.title: the virtual explosion of Ladakh studies since the early 1980s

data.event: But after that, despite the virtual explosion of Ladakh studies since the early 1980s, scholarly attention has been to a large extent concentrated on the fields of ecology and agriculture, social anthropology, history and religious art, and the oral tradition has been relatively neglected, especially by scholars from outside.

event.title: summer, the fact that the sun does shine

data.event: Emotionally, because in this climate of extreme winter cold, and a short summer, the fact that the sun does shine, many more days than not, even in the depth of winter, must surely contribute enormously to making life not only bearable but on the whole happy.

event.title: ported from there into Ladakh, particularly during the hundred years

data.event: Very different is the ghazal, the classical form of Urdu and Persian poetry, a variant of which developed in Baltistan and was im­ ported from there into Ladakh, particularly during the hundred years or so of the Dogra raj when Ladakh and Baltistan were yoked together administratively, and there was constant inter­ course between them.

event.title: the creation of the world, and of its early days

data.event: Essentially it is a collection of nature myths, and purports to tell the story of the creation of the world, and of its early days when it was peopled by wicked giants, and authentic, though endearingly fallible, heroes.

event.title: the departure of life and warmth from the earth in winter

data.event: This, the abduction of Bruguma by the king of Hor, Kesar’s return to remembrance and his rescue of Bruguma have been interpreted — as have many of the lesser anecdotes in the Saga — to be a myth of the departure of life and warmth from the earth in winter, and their return in spring.

event.title: — a good two thousand years ago

data.event: Although the Kesar Saga must have had its origins well before the introduction of Buddhism into Tibet — a good two thousand years ago, most likely — its different versions have been updated at various times and places by the accretions inseparable from the process of oral transmission.

event.title: took centuries

data.event: But, imposed from above by the rulers probably for political reasons, it took centuries to be whole­ heartedly accepted by the people; and up to recent times so marked were the traces of Buddhism that Chigtan might be described as having been no more than half Islamized.

event.title: left or died more than a hundred years ago

data.event: The last lama to be custodian of the Chigtan gompa left or died more than a hundred years ago; and in 1909 Francke found the temple being looked after by two Muslims, the representatives, they told him, of the family of the former Buddhist custodians, who were punctilious in lighting the butter-lamps and attending to the images.

data.event: These poems, or others like them, would in the past have been part of the mental equipment of every Ladakhi.

data.event: Whether this remains true today is difficult to say.

event.title: today threatened

data.event: 1 0 Change T he balance with the natural environment which is the basic characteristic of Ladakhi life is today threatened by the forces of change and so-called progress which no ancient society, brought face to face with the modern world, can hope to escape.

event.title: centuries by a process of constant refine­ ment of response to the existing climatic and environmental con­ ditions; in the case of Ladakh, these involve

data.event: As with all long-established pre-modern societies, that of Ladakh has evolved over centuries by a process of constant refine­ ment of response to the existing climatic and environmental con­ ditions; in the case of Ladakh, these involve severe limitations as regards both the climate and the carrying capacity of the land.

event.title: evolved by painful trial and error over centuries

data.event: The Ladakhis’ methods of meeting the challenges posed by their inhospitable environment — methods evolved by painful trial and error over centuries if not millennia — have stood the test of time.

event.title: the past render the country self-sufficient in foodgrains —

data.event: It is probably true that, whatever the excellence of its methods and its results, Ladakhi agriculture — whether or not it did in the past render the country self-sufficient in foodgrains — can no longer by itself support today’s growing population.

event.title: support today

data.event: It is probably true that, whatever the excellence of its methods and its results, Ladakhi agriculture — whether or not it did in the past render the country self-sufficient in foodgrains — can no longer by itself support today’s growing population.

event.title: provides a cushion for the poor, especially in a year

data.event: And it is equally true that on the whole the Ladakhis themselves, especially the less well-off, welcome the Public Distribution System, which surely provides a cushion for the poor, especially in a year of bad harvest.

event.title: days too, rivers proved

data.event: In the old days too, rivers proved formidable obstacles, often swirling through deep chasms, almost always rushing too impetuously over their stony beds to make fording safe or easy.

event.title: ’ tales of the last century

data.event: Travellers’ tales of the last century are full of exciting accounts of river-crossings by rope-bridges swaying precariously over the foaming torrent beneath.

event.title: planned development, some three-quarters of all the villages of the two districts are now

data.event: After twenty-five years of planned development, some three-quarters of all the villages of the two districts are now linked by a network of roads, most of them not metalled, it is true, but fit for heavy-duty vehicles; and the jhoola bridges of the old days are being replaced by steel girders.

data.event: 176 LADAKH inception, and for many years the local people regarded it as little more than a bad joke.

event.title: went on line in 1995

data.event: At Kargil town, the long talked-about 3.75 megawatt Iqbal Power Project went on line in 1995; and there are not only plans for a giant 12-megawatt project at Chutuk, a village a few kilometres up the Suru from Kargil, but even talk of a 60-megawatt monster in the same region.

event.title: winter, Leh is served

data.event: In winter, Leh is served by an old and noisy diesel-generating station, as are some villages without a convenient source of hydroelectricity; in others, where the dwellings are very scattered, individual solar photo-voltaic conversion sets are being installed.

data.event: This startlingly high-tech programme was initiated by a Rajas­ than-based voluntary agency, the Social Work and Research Cen­ tre (SWRC), Tilonia; it has been so successful that it has now been taken up seriously by the administrations of the two districts.

event.title: now have after-dark lighting, powered

data.event: In the two districts nearly 3000 houses in the remotest areas now have after-dark lighting, powered by solar energy converted by photo-voltaic cells and stored in batteries, sufficient to run two light-points for about four hours; while the tent-dwelling Chang- pa are provided with similar solar-powered lanterns.

data.event: One particularly popular innovation has been the low-cost greenhouses — little more than sheets of heavy-duty UV-resistant polythene stretched over a low brick-wall infrastructure — of which several thousand have already been installed, with the aim that by the end of the century they will be available to every interested household.

event.title: the late 1970s the Agriculture Department established

data.event: In the late 1970s the Agriculture Department established a number of plots for the multiplication of its seed; twenty years later the seed production on private fields is measured in tens of quintals, and the area under ol rose from 26 hectares in 1981—2 to 1600 hectares in 1995-6.

data.event: It gave two lambs a year and its great advantage was that it could be kept at practically no expense, as it was happy to eat almost anything — leaves, grass, straw, household refuse like vegetable parings and spent tea-leaves; Moorcroft even saw one nibble a bone.

data.event: From at least the seventeenth century the trade in pashm was controlled in favour of a caucus of Kashmiri middlemen, known as Tibet Baqals; even in the post-Independence era, the State Government tried, by means of its Pashmina Control Order, to maintain the salient feature of the system established under the Treaty of Tingmosgang in 1684 — namely that there should be no movement of pashm outside Jammu and Kashmir.

event.title: months of the agricultural year, and thus takes

data.event: The peak season for tourism-related work coincides with the busiest months of the agricultural year, and thus takes the young men away from their villages just at the time when their labour is most needed.

event.title: the relative difficulty of access — flights in the tourist season being often fully booked weeks

data.event: Ladakh’s altitude and remoteness, and the relative difficulty of access — flights in the tourist season being often fully booked weeks if not months in advance, while the 19-hour drive from Manali is a physical ordeal for all but the most fit — has tended to ensure that it gets a higher proportion of what might be described as ‘quality tourists’ than, say, Manali or Goa or Kath­ mandu.

event.title: the first few years after Ladakh was opened

data.event: This is not to discount entirely the fear, cogently expressed by sympathetic observers in the first few years after Ladakh was opened to outsiders, that tourism would result in cultural erosion and psychological disorientation; a fear based on observed changes in people’s attitudes.3 Even now there must be Ladakhis who react with anxiety and confusion to the pressures of modernization in general and tourism in particular.

event.title: life and work; now

data.event: To give just one example, changes in the village economy have a tendency to erode co-opera­ tive patterns of life and work; now increasingly when extra labour is needed for the spring sowing or for taking in the harvest, even local helpers expect a wage, rather than assuming a reciprocal arrangement.

event.title: exploit the free and renewable sources of energy that are so abundant in Ladakh, and are only now

data.event: There are many both inside and outside the region who believe that the answer may lie in the LEDeG approach: do not assume that because a certain model of economics or technology has worked (if it has) in the Western world it is ipso facto appropriate for Ladakh; decentralize your decision-making and your economic structures; minimize your reliance for vital economic inputs on outside sources of supply over which you have no control; exploit the free and renewable sources of energy that are so abundant in Ladakh, and are only now being recognized by the conventional ‘developers’ as offering viable possibilities; remember that all systems and all processes are interdependent, and assess the impact that change in any one of them may have on all the others; understanding the relationship between technology and social structures, never fall into the trap of believing that because a technological in­ novation reduces someone’s workload or improves their produc­ tivity, it is necessarily an overall improvement; and introduce any change only after the fullest discussion with those whose lives are going to be affected by it.

data.event: Although on the whole relations among the three communities have been amicable, particularly in comparison with the com­ munal feeling experienced often so bitterly in other parts of India, they have in the last few decades of the twentieth century tended to get politicized, and this has, sadly, led to friction and actual violence.

data.event: The seeds of communal feeling were sown as early as 1931, when some Kashmiri neo-Buddhists took up the cause of the Buddhists of Ladakh, and founded the Kashmir Raj Bodhi Mahasabha to give them a voice as a distinct community.

data.event: Politics over the first few decades of Inde­ pendence had reference largely to issues on which Buddhists and Muslims had no fundamental differences; but the bifurcation of the region into two administrative districts in 1979 — Buddhist- majority Leh and Muslim-majority Kargil — again brought the communal question to the fore.

event.title: spearheaded by the Ladakh Buddhist Association in 1989

data.event: Insensitive handling of perceived grievances by the Muslim-dominated State Government resulted in an agitation spearheaded by the Ladakh Buddhist Association in 1989, which took an unfortunately anti-Muslim as well as anti-Government turn; the fall-out was a social rift between the two communities in Leh which was not healed till 1993.

data.event: The main du-khang, a large rather bare temple, has images of Stag-tsang-ras-pa and other Drug-pa lamas, together with a fine silver chorten about fifty years old made at Chiling.

event.title: centuries, while even the uneven stone floor is grimy, and feels

data.event: This, made into a temple, is an excessively dark and gloomy chamber, the low rocky ceiling and the walls being absolutely black from the smoke of the numberless butter- lamps that have burned there over the centuries, while even the uneven stone floor is grimy, and feels sticky to the touch of bare feet.

event.title: was consecrated by His Holiness the Dalai Lama in September 1980

data.event: A new temple a litde way below the main gompa complex was consecrated by His Holiness the Dalai Lama in September 1980.

event.title: entered holy orders in 1630

data.event: Associated with the gompa s early history is the name of Nawang Namgyal, one of Sengge’s half-brothers, the sons of Jamyang Namgyal’s first marriage, who entered holy orders in 1630.

event.title: manufactured in Chiling in the middle decades

data.event: A small courtyard has steps leading up to the du-kbang, whose principal feature is a silver chorten manufactured in Chiling in the middle decades of this century — an object of exquisite craftsmanship studded with great flawless turquoises.

event.title: built about the early 1970s

data.event: There is however a new du-khang, built about the early 1970s, the verandah of which has paintings of the Lords of the Quarters in an unusually plain and sober rendering.

event.title: takes place around the Buddhist new year, usually in the first half of March

data.event: Matho Gompa is famous for its annual festival of the oracles which takes place around the Buddhist new year, usually in the first half of March, The oracles are two lamas, chosen by lot every three years who, when purified by months of fasting and medita­ tion, become the receptacles for the spirit of a particular deity.

event.title: the Dogra conquest, now

data.event: Stok Palace Museum Stok Palace, seat of the Namgyal family after they had been deprived of their royal authority by the Dogra conquest, now houses a museum in which the family’s heirlooms and relics are displayed, a poignant evocation of Ladakh’s vanished past as an independent kingdom.

event.title: the great Balti invasion about 1600

data.event: It is mentioned as Ladakh’s capital in the first European account of Ladakh, by the Portuguese merchant Diogo d’Almeida just before the great Balti invasion about 1600 and it was at Basgo, strategically situated where the near approach of the mountains to the river turns the Indus valley into a gorge, that the advance of the invading Tibetan and Mongol armies was checked in 1680.

event.title: withstood for three years

data.event: Ruined fortifications bear witness to the palace’s strength against siege, which it withstood for three years at that time, being said to have enjoyed a perennial water-supply.

event.title: now a heap of rubble, barely distin­ guishable from the stones of the encroaching desert), one of the few monasteries known

data.event: He was a graduate of Nyar-ma (near Tikse; now a heap of rubble, barely distin­ guishable from the stones of the encroaching desert), one of the few monasteries known for certain to have been founded by Rin-chen-zang-po.

event.title: will find in attendance today

data.event: Respon­ sibility for its maintenance was assumed at some point by the Ge-lugs-pa monastery of Likir, and it is Likir monks whom the visitor will find in attendance today.

event.title: the time of the Second Spreading — that is, in the eleventh and twelfth centuries

data.event: Thus we have at Alchi a remarkable record of Buddhist icono­ graphy at the time of the Second Spreading — that is, in the eleventh and twelfth centuries — unrivalled except for Tabo.

event.title: had fallen on evil days

data.event: The Lotsawa Lha-khang, as its name implies, has an image, and also a painting of the Great Translator, Rin-chen- zang-po; these are rarities, as this scholar whose work was so crucial for the revival of Buddhism in Tibet when it had fallen on evil days, is noticeably absent in the later gompas.

event.title: centuries of quiet medita­ tion and worship, did their successors abandon

data.event: And for what reason, after centuries of quiet medita­ tion and worship, did their successors abandon the marvellous monument they had raised, in this setting seemingly so perfect for the contemplative life?

event.title: was founded only a hundred years ago

data.event: Ri-dzong The ‘reformed’ gompa of Ri-dzong was founded only a hundred years ago, to give full expression to the monastic ideal of the Ge-lugs-pa.

event.title: is served to each monk separately in his own quarters

data.event: This is the only monastery in which they do not have individual cooking arrangements, but eat from a communal kitchen, though not all together in a dining hall or refectory; the food is served to each monk separately in his own quarters.

event.title: come up to the gompa once or twice a month

data.event: The chomos come up to the gompa once or twice a month for special pujas, otherwise they remain in the precincts of the chomoling, conducting the everyday services themselves.

event.title: now much worn and defaced

data.event: The carved doorway, and the paintings, now much worn and defaced, are distinctly reminiscent of Alchi.

event.title: made only a few years ago

data.event: The main image in the du-khang, as well as in the inner shrine behind the main altar, is of the Dri-gung-pa guru, Skyoba Jigsten Gonbo; also in the inner shrine is a modern set of the twenty-one manifestations of Tara, in copper-gilt, made only a few years ago in Aligarh.

event.title: are not painted, though the verandah has the usual Lords of the Quarters

data.event: The inside walls are not painted, though the verandah has the usual Lords of the Quarters and Wheel of life, in obviously recent renderings.

event.title: reached only after days

data.event: The Mountain Gompas: The Mulbekh Gompas Deep in the folds of the Zanskar range, reached only after days on the mountain trail, are several gompas of great antiquity.

event.title: now abandoned gompa, said

data.event: Further along the same trail, the village of Photoksar has a now abandoned gompa, said still to contain a few images; while shortly before reaching Lamayuru the route passes the Wanla Gompa, with its image of eleven-headed Avalokiteswara.

event.title: quarters tucked

data.event: It consists of no more than a single temple with, presumably, the lama’s living quarters tucked away somewhere at the back.

event.title: was built in 1975

data.event: It was built in 1975; the Ngari Rinpoche to whom it is dedicated is the Dalai Lama’s younger brother, the head lama of Likir Gompa.

event.title: quarters, and a tiny chapel, pardy carved

data.event: Once at the top, there is nothing but a verandah, which serves as the lama’s living quarters, and a tiny chapel, pardy carved out of the rock itself, with images of Sakyamuni and eleven-headed Avalokiteswara.

event.title: religious practice from perhaps the early centuries

data.event: While these are indications of a site that has been associated with religious practice from perhaps the early centuries of the Christian era, the gompa itself is said to have been founded by Padmasambhava, to whom is dedicated the main temple, the Guru Lha-khang.

event.title: became the head­ quarters

data.event: Appendix The Jesuits in Western Tibet I n 1625 Tsaparang, the capital of Guge, became the head­ quarters of a Jesuit mission under the Portuguese priest, An­ tonio de Andrade.

event.title: days of Nyima-Gon, founder of the first Ladakhi dynasty, Guge had been part of the Ladakhi empire; later allotted

data.event: In the days of Nyima-Gon, founder of the first Ladakhi dynasty, Guge had been part of the Ladakhi empire; later allotted to one of Nyima-Gon’s younger sons, it became a kind of vassal state of Ladakh.

event.title: is situated on the upper waters of the Sudej, now

data.event: Tsaparang is situated on the upper waters of the Sudej, now a few kilometres inside the Tibetan border.

event.title: 1624, Andrade, one of the priests in residence at the court of Jahangir, found

data.event: In 1624, Andrade, one of the priests in residence at the court of Jahangir, found an opportunity to test the truth of these rumours by travelling into the fastness of the Himalaya.

event.title: left Tsaparang at the end of August

data.event: He left Tsaparang at the end of August with a written commission from the king appointing him Chief Lama, and giving him ‘full authority to teach the holy law to our people’.

event.title: set out again in June 1625

data.event: He was back in Agra early in November with two boys from Tsaparang; and having obtained the necessary sanction from the Provincial Head of the Jesuits at Goa, set out again in June 1625, arriving at Tsaparang in the third week of August.

event.title: his promises of the previous year

data.event: The Guge king fulfilled his promises of the previous year, and himself laid the foundation stone of the church of Our Lady of Hope.

event.title: about five years the missionaries, never more than five of them, pursued

data.event: For about five years the missionaries, never more than five of them, pursued a strange and lonely existence in this petty Tibetan capital, which cannot have been much more than a rude village; however all difficulties were smoothed away by the en­ thusiastic patronage of the king.

event.title: had happened years

data.event: This had happened years before Andrade arrived, but the facts would have been common knowledge during his time in Tsaparang, and there is no reason to doubt the accuracy of his report.

event.title: had been suffering from the depredations of the Ladakhi army for eighteen years

data.event: His story implies that the Ladakh king had made this slight the pretext for making war on Guge, which had been suffering from the depredations of the Ladakhi army for eighteen years.

event.title: now ensued

data.event: The presence of Jesuits seems to have had a catalytic effect, helping to precipitate the crisis that now ensued.

data.event: The Tsaparang mission never recovered from this catastrophe, and was finally closed down in 1635, though a watch was kept on the situation till about 1640 to see whether there was any possibility of resuming operations.

event.title: left Tsaparang on 4 October

data.event: They left Tsaparang on 4 October, when the season was already well advanced; and reached Leh on 25 October, after a journey for whose hardships even the severity of the Mana Pass could have been little preparation.

event.title: reached Leh on 25 October

data.event: They left Tsaparang on 4 October, when the season was already well advanced; and reached Leh on 25 October, after a journey for whose hardships even the severity of the Mana Pass could have been little preparation.

data.event: (Ibid.) The two priests were received courteously, and regaled with butter-tea and tsampa Although the Tsaparang mission was not able to recover from the disastrous events of 1630, Sengge did send Azevedo and Oliveira away with kind words, and a promise that missionaries might continue their work in Guge, Rudok and even in Leh.

event.title: fifteen days, the two priests left

data.event: After a stay of only fifteen days, the two priests left Leh on 7 November — a terrible season to travel far over those bleak and bitterly cold and upland wastes.

event.title: 7 November —

data.event: After a stay of only fifteen days, the two priests left Leh on 7 November — a terrible season to travel far over those bleak and bitterly cold and upland wastes.

event.title: faced on this gruelling trek, and the next day

data.event: At this point Azevedo nearly died; but it was the last of the perils they faced on this gruelling trek, and the next day they descended to the gentle landscape of the Kulu valley.

event.title: reached Agra on 3 January 1632

data.event: They reached Agra on 3 January 1632, having accomplished the journey from Leh in just short of two months.

event.title: lay in the obscurity of the archives of the Society of Jesus in Rome until 1924

data.event: The story of the Tsaparang mission and Azevedo’s visit to Leh was unknown to nineteenth-century writers on Ladakh, for the records lay in the obscurity of the archives of the Society of Jesus in Rome until 1924 when C. Wessels SJ published his Early Jesuit Travellers in Central Asia.

event.title: did not arise till 1714

data.event: The opportunity did not arise till 1714 when two priests, an Italian, Ippolito Desideri and a Portuguese, Emanuel Freyre, set off from Delhi over the Pir Panjal into Kashmir en route for Tibet.

event.title: set off towards the end of May

data.event: They wintered in Srinagar and set off towards the end of May 1715 to cross the Zoji-la, which they did on 30 May in a blizzard.

event.title: now Ladakh’s

data.event: They were the first Europeans to travel the line which is now Ladakh’s main link with the world south of the Himalaya; but

event.title: did not reach Leh till 20 June

data.event: They did not reach Leh till 20 June, which means that their journey took considerably longer than the sixteen days between Srinagar and Leh reckoned by travellers of the nineteenth century and indeed up till the time of the construction of the motor road in the 1960s.

data.event: He overruled Desideri, and after a two-month stay in Leh, they left like Azevedo at the beginning of winter, reaching Lhasa seven months later.

event.title: death and had now

data.event: From the Tibetan border onwards, their party was attached to the entourage of a beautiful Tartar princess, widow of the late military governor of Gartok, who had taken over the command on her husband’s death and had now been recalled to Lhasa.

event.title: crawled across the interminable steppes in the biting cold of winter

data.event: In spite of this unlooked-for good fortune one wonders if Freyre, a man at once weak-willed and cantankerous, ever had any doubts about the wisdom of his decision to travel east, as the caravan crawled across the interminable steppes in the biting cold of winter.

event.title: remained for five years

data.event: He left Lhasa almost immediately to return to the plains of Hindus­ tan, but Desideri remained for five years, witnessing and recording the dramatic political events which laid the foundation of Chinese claims to suzerainty over Tibet, and immersing himself in the study of Buddhism, the better to refute its ‘false doctrines’.

event.title: given by Moorcroft a hundred years later

data.event: Obviously, Desideri’s account of Ladakh is by no means the most interesting or important part of his Relazione, and in insight and fullness of detail it falls far short of the one given by Moorcroft a hundred years later.

event.title: — ‘two months

data.event: He first describes the extent of the country — ‘two months’ journey in length’ — and enumerates the territories on its borders.

event.title: know of Ladakh today

data.event: (Desideri, An Account of Tibet) This corroborates at almost every point both what we know of Ladakh today, and the scattered hints we have relating to its history.

event.title: is transferred to the heir, on his or her reaching years

data.event: Lit., little house: social custom by which the ownership of a piece of property is transferred to the heir, on his or her reaching years of discretion, the parents retiring to a little house adjoining the main property, keeping only enough land and live­ stock for their own sustenance.

event.title: the time of the actual Tibetan new year, towards the end of February

data.event: Celebrated in Ladakh not at the time of the actual Tibetan new year, towards the end of February or early March, but on the first day of the eleventh lunar month, usually in December.
